title: British Film Institute

infobox.value: 21 Stephen Street, London, United Kingdom W1T 1LN

(**BFI**) is a film and television charitable organisation which promotes and preserves [independent](/wiki/Independent_cinema_in_the_United_Kingdom "Independent cinema in the United Kingdom") and [mainstream filmmaking](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom") and [television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The BFI uses funds provided by the [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)") to encourage film production, distribution, and education. It is sponsored by the [Department for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Culture, Media and Sport"),[[2]](#cite_note-gov-2) and partially funded under the [British Film Institute Act 1949](/wiki/British_Film_Institute_Act_1949 "British Film Institute Act 1949").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British film archive and charity

"BFI" redirects here. For other uses, see [BFI (disambiguation)](/wiki/BFI_(disambiguation) "BFI (disambiguation)").

British Film Institute

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Map | |
| Abbreviation | BFI |
| Predecessor | [UK Film Council](/wiki/UK_Film_Council "UK Film Council") |
| Formation | 1933; 93 years ago (1933) |
| Type | Film, television charitable organisation |
| Headquarters | 21 Stephen Street, London, United Kingdom W1T 1LN |
| Region served | [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") |
| Chair | [Jay Hunt](/wiki/Jay_Hunt_(television_executive) "Jay Hunt (television executive)") |
| Chief Executive | Ben Roberts |
| Revenue | £127,827,000[[1]](#cite_note-1) (2022–23) |
| Website | [www.bfi.org.uk](https://www.bfi.org.uk) |

The **British Film Institute** (**BFI**) is a film and television charitable organisation which promotes and preserves [independent](/wiki/Independent_cinema_in_the_United_Kingdom "Independent cinema in the United Kingdom") and [mainstream filmmaking](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom") and [television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The BFI uses funds provided by the [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)") to encourage film production, distribution, and education. It is sponsored by the [Department for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Culture, Media and Sport"),[[2]](#cite_note-gov-2) and partially funded under the [British Film Institute Act 1949](/wiki/British_Film_Institute_Act_1949 "British Film Institute Act 1949").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Activities")]

### Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Purpose")]

The BFI was established in 1933 to encourage the development of the arts of [film](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom"), [television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom") and the moving image throughout the UK to promote their use as a record of contemporary life and manners, to promote education about film, television and the moving image generally, and their impact on society, to promote access to and appreciation of the widest possible range of British and [world cinema](/wiki/World_cinema "World cinema") and to establish, care for and develop collections reflecting the moving image history, heritage and [culture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Archive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Archive")]

The BFI maintains the world's largest [film archive](/wiki/Film_archive "Film archive"), the [BFI National Archive](/wiki/BFI_National_Archive "BFI National Archive"), previously called [National Film Library](/wiki/National_Film_Library "National Film Library") (1935–1955), National Film Archive (1955–1992), and National Film and Television Archive (1993–2006). The archive contains more than 50,000 fiction films, over 100,000 non-fiction titles, and around 625,000 television programmes. The majority of the collection is British material but it also features internationally significant holdings from around the world. The archive also holds documents which feature key actors and the work of [film makers](/wiki/Film_director "Film director").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Cinemas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cinemas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/BFI_London_IMAX_at_night.jpg/250px-BFI_London_IMAX_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:BFI_London_IMAX_at_night.jpg)

[BFI IMAX](/wiki/BFI_IMAX "BFI IMAX") cinema

The BFI runs the [BFI Southbank](/wiki/BFI_Southbank "BFI Southbank") (formerly the National Film Theatre (NFT)) and the [BFI IMAX](/wiki/BFI_IMAX "BFI IMAX") cinema, both located on the south bank of the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") in London.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The IMAX has the largest cinema screen in the UK and shows popular recent releases and short films showcasing its technology, which includes IMAX 70mm screenings, IMAX 3D screenings and 11,600 watts of digital surround sound.[[7]](#cite_note-7) BFI Southbank shows films from all over the world, particularly critically acclaimed historical and specialised films that may not otherwise get a cinema showing. The BFI also distributes archival and cultural cinema to other venues – each year to more than 800 venues all across the UK, as well as to a substantial number of overseas venues.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Education")]

The BFI offers a range of education initiatives, in particular to support the teaching of film and media studies in schools.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In late 2012, the BFI received money from the [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education") to create the BFI Film Academy Network for young people aged between 16 and 25.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) A residential scheme is held at the [National Film and Television School](/wiki/National_Film_and_Television_School "National Film and Television School") (NFTS) every year.

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Festivals")]

The BFI runs the annual [London Film Festival](/wiki/BFI_London_Film_Festival "BFI London Film Festival") along with [BFI Flare: London LGBTIQ+ Film Festival](/wiki/BFI_Flare:_London_LGBTIQ%2B_Film_Festival "BFI Flare: London LGBTIQ+ Film Festival") and the youth-orientated [Future Film Festival](/wiki/BFI_Future_Film_Festival "BFI Future Film Festival").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Other activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other activities")]

:   *See also [Category:British Film Institute films](/wiki/Category:British_Film_Institute_films "Category:British Film Institute films")*

The BFI publishes the monthly *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")* magazine, as well as films on [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), DVD and books. It runs the BFI Reuben Library (a free reference library open to the public at [BFI Southbank](/wiki/BFI_Southbank "BFI Southbank")), and maintains the [BFI Film & TV Database](/wiki/BFI_Film_%26_TV_Database "BFI Film & TV Database") and Summary of Information on Film and Television (SIFT), which are databases of credits, synopses and other information about film and television productions. SIFT has a collection of about 7 million [still frames](/wiki/Still_frame "Still frame") from film and television.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The BFI has co-produced a number of television series featuring footage from the BFI National Archive, in partnership with the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), including *[The Lost World of Mitchell & Kenyon](/wiki/The_Lost_World_of_Mitchell_%26_Kenyon "The Lost World of Mitchell & Kenyon")*, *[The Lost World of Friese-Greene](/wiki/The_Lost_World_of_Friese-Greene "The Lost World of Friese-Greene")* and *[The Lost World of Tibet](/wiki/The_Lost_World_of_Tibet "The Lost World of Tibet")*.

The BFI has also produced contemporary artists' moving image work, most notably through the programme of the [BFI Gallery](/wiki/BFI_Gallery "BFI Gallery"), which was located at [BFI Southbank](/wiki/BFI_Southbank "BFI Southbank") from March 2007 to March 2011. The programme of the gallery resulted in several new commissions by leading artists, including projects which engaged directly with the BFI National Archive, among which are [Patrick Keiller](/wiki/Patrick_Keiller "Patrick Keiller")'s 'The City of the Future', [Iain Forsyth and Jane Pollard](/wiki/Iain_Forsyth_and_Jane_Pollard "Iain Forsyth and Jane Pollard")'s 'RadioMania: An Abandoned Work' and Deimantas Narkevicious' 'Into the Unknown'. The Gallery also initiated projects by filmmakers such as [Michael Snow](/wiki/Michael_Snow "Michael Snow"), [Apichatpong Weerasethakul](/wiki/Apichatpong_Weerasethakul "Apichatpong Weerasethakul"), [Jane and Louise Wilson](/wiki/Jane_and_Louise_Wilson "Jane and Louise Wilson") and [John Akomfrah](/wiki/John_Akomfrah "John Akomfrah").[[14]](#cite_note-gallery_book-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The BFI also operates a streaming service, BFI Player. This streaming service offers a variety of niche and art films.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Organisation")]

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/National_Film_Theatre_%28post-1957_logo%29.jpg/250px-National_Film_Theatre_%28post-1957_logo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Film_Theatre_(post-1957_logo).jpg)

[National Film Theatre](/wiki/BFI_Southbank "BFI Southbank")

The institute was founded in 1933.[[2]](#cite_note-gov-2) Despite its foundation resulting from a recommendation in a report on *Film in National Life*, at that time the institute was a private company, though it has received public money throughout its history. This came from the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council") and Treasury until 1965, and from the various culture departments since then.

The institute was restructured following the Radcliffe Report of 1948, which recommended that it should concentrate on developing the appreciation of filmic art, rather than creating film itself. Thus control of educational film production passed to the National Committee for Visual Aids in Education and the [British Film Academy](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts") assumed control for promoting production. From 1952 to 2000, the BFI provided funding for new and experimental film-makers via the [BFI Production Board](/wiki/BFI_Production_Board "BFI Production Board").

The institute received a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") in 1983. This was updated in 2000, and in the same year the newly established [UK Film Council](/wiki/UK_Film_Council "UK Film Council") took responsibility for providing the BFI's annual grant-in-aid (government subsidy). As an independent registered charity, the BFI is regulated by the [Charity Commission](/wiki/Charity_Commission "Charity Commission") and the Privy Council.

In 1988, the BFI opened the London [Museum of the Moving Image](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Moving_Image_(London) "Museum of the Moving Image (London)") (MOMI) on the [South Bank](/wiki/South_Bank "South Bank"). MOMI was acclaimed internationally and set new standards for education through entertainment, but it did not receive the high levels of continuing investment that might have enabled it to keep pace with technological developments and ever-rising audience expectations. The museum was "temporarily" closed in 1999 when the BFI stated that it would be re-sited. This did not happen, and MOMI's closure became permanent in 2002 when it was decided to redevelop the South Bank site. This redevelopment was itself then further delayed.

### Today

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=British_Film_Institute&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Today")]

The BFI is managed on a day-to-day basis by its chief executive, Ben Roberts. Supreme decision-making authority rests with a chair and a board of up to 15 governors. The current chair is [Jay Hunt](/wiki/Jay_Hunt_(television_executive) "Jay Hunt (television executive)"), a television executive, who took up the post in February 2024.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Governors, including the Chair, are appointed by the [Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport").

The BFI operates with three sources of income. The largest is public money allocated by the [Department for Culture, Media and Sport](/wiki/Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport "Department for Culture, Media and Sport"). For the year 2021–22, the BFI received £74.31m from the DCMS as Grant-in-Aid funding.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The second largest source is commercial activity such as receipts from ticket sales at BFI Southbank or the BFI London IMAX theatre (£5m in 2007), sales of DVDs, etc. Thirdly, grants and sponsorship of around £5m are obtained from various sources, including [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)") funding grants, private sponsors and through donations ([J. Paul Getty, Jr.](/wiki/Paul_Getty "Paul Getty"), who died in 2003, left the BFI a legacy of around £1m in his will). The BFI is also the distributor for all Lottery funds for film (in 2011–12 this amounted to c.£25m).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As well as its work on film, the BFI also devotes a large amount of its time to the preservation and study of British television programming and its history. In 2000, it published a high-profile list of the [100 Greatest British Television Programmes](/wiki/100_Greatest_British_Television_Programmes "100 Greatest British Television Programmes"), as voted for by a range of industry figures.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The delayed redevelopment of the National Film Theatre finally took place in 2007, creating in the rebranded "BFI Southbank" new education spaces, a contemporary art gallery dedicated to the moving image[[14]](#cite_note-gallery_book-14) (the [BFI Gallery](/wiki/BFI_Gallery "BFI Gallery")), and a pioneering mediatheque which for the first time enabled the public to gain access, free of charge, to some of the otherwise inaccessible treasures in the National Film & Television Archive. The mediatheque has proved to be the most successful element of this redevelopment, and there are plans to roll out a network of them across the UK.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An announcement of a £25 million capital investment in the Strategy for UK Screen Heritage was made by Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport at the opening night of the 2007 London Film Festival. The bulk of this money paid for long overdue development of the BFI National Archive facilities in [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire") and [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During 2009, the [UK Film Council](/wiki/UK_Film_Council "UK Film Council") persuaded the government that there should only be one main public-funded body for film, and that body should be the UKFC, while the BFI should be abolished. In 2010, the government announced that there would be a single body for film. Despite intensive lobbying (including, controversially, using public funding to pay public relations agencies to put its case forward), the UKFC failed to persuade the government that it should have that role and, instead, the BFI took over most of the UKFC's functions and funding from 1 April 2011, with the UKFC being subsequently abolished. Since then, the BFI has been responsible for all Lottery funding for film—originally in excess of £25m p.a., and currently in excess of £40m p.a.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The BFI Film Academy forms part of the BFI's overall 5–19 Education Scheme. The programme is being supported by the [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education") in England who have committed £1m per annum funding from April 2012 and 31 March 2015. It is also funded through the [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)"), [Creative Scotland](/wiki/Creative_Scotland "Creative Scotland") and [Northern Ireland Screen](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Screen "Northern Ireland Screen").

On 29 November 2016, the BFI announced that over 100,000 television programmes are to be [digitised](/wiki/Digitization "Digitization") before the video tapes, which currently have an estimated five-to-six-year shelf life, become unusable. The BFI aims to make sure that the television archive is still there in 200 years' time.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The BFI announced in February 2021 that it is teaming up with American diversity and inclusion program #StartWith8Hollywood founded by [Thuc Doan Nguyen](/wiki/Thuc_Doan_Nguyen "Thuc Doan Nguyen") to make it global.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
